questions.scenario: Two haploid gametes fuse.
questions.stem: The cell formed is a:
questions.explanation: Haploid gametes fuse to form a diploid zygote, the basis of inheritance in eukaryotes with a sexual life cycle.

questions.stem: A diploid cell has how many copies of each autosomal gene?
questions.explanation: A diploid cell has two copies of each autosomal gene, one from each parent.

questions.scenario: In a genetic cross, the first offspring generation is produced.
questions.stem: This generation is called the:
questions.explanation: The parental (P) generation produces the F1 generation, which can be crossed to give the F2.

questions.scenario: A student predicts offspring ratios using a grid.
questions.stem: This grid is called a:
questions.explanation: A Punnett grid is used to predict the genotypes and phenotypes of offspring from a cross.

questions.scenario: An organism has two identical alleles of a gene.
questions.stem: Its genotype is:
questions.explanation: Homozygous means having two identical alleles; heterozygous means two different alleles.

questions.stem: The difference between a gene and an allele is that an allele is:

options.text: A specific version of a gene

questions.explanation: A gene is a length of DNA coding for a characteristic; an allele is a particular version of that gene.

questions.stem: The phenotype of an organism results from:
questions.explanation: Phenotype is the observable traits resulting from genotype and environmental factors.

questions.scenario: A trait is controlled by a dominant allele (T) and a recessive allele (t).
questions.stem: Which genotypes give the dominant phenotype?
questions.explanation: Both homozygous-dominant (TT) and heterozygous (Tt) genotypes produce the dominant phenotype.

questions.stem: A recessive phenotype is only shown when the genotype is:
questions.explanation: A recessive allele is only expressed in the phenotype when homozygous recessive.

questions.scenario: An organism changes its traits to suit its environment by varying gene expression, and the change can reverse.
questions.stem: This is called:
questions.explanation: Phenotypic plasticity is the capacity to develop traits suited to the environment by varying gene expression, and it may be reversible.

questions.stem: Phenotypic plasticity is NOT due to:
questions.explanation: Phenotypic plasticity is not due to changes in genotype; it arises from varying gene expression in response to the environment.

questions.scenario: Phenylketonuria (PKU) is caused by a mutation in an autosomal gene.
questions.stem: PKU is inherited as a:
questions.explanation: PKU is a recessive genetic condition caused by a mutation in an autosomal gene.

questions.scenario: PKU results from a faulty enzyme.
questions.stem: The affected enzyme normally converts:
questions.explanation: PKU is caused by a mutation in the gene for the enzyme that converts phenylalanine to tyrosine.

questions.scenario: A gene has several alleles in the gene pool.
questions.stem: How many of these alleles can one individual inherit?
questions.explanation: Any number of alleles can exist in a gene pool, but an individual inherits only two.

questions.scenario: The ABO blood group system is controlled by multiple alleles.
questions.stem: Which alleles are used to denote it?
questions.explanation: The ABO blood group is an example of multiple alleles, denoted IA, IB and i.

questions.scenario: A heterozygote shows both alleles fully in its phenotype (e.g. AB blood type).
questions.stem: This pattern of inheritance is:
questions.explanation: In codominance, heterozygotes have a dual phenotype; AB blood type (IAIB) is an example.

questions.scenario: A red-flowered and a white-flowered plant produce pink offspring.
questions.stem: This pattern of inheritance is:
questions.explanation: In incomplete dominance, heterozygotes have an intermediate phenotype, as in the pink four o'clock flower.

questions.scenario: In humans, the sex chromosome carried by the sperm determines the offspring's sex.
questions.stem: This is because the sperm can carry:

options.text: Either an X or a Y chromosome

questions.explanation: The sex chromosome in the sperm (X or Y) determines whether the zygote develops male- or female-typical characteristics.

questions.stem: Compared with the Y chromosome, the X chromosome carries:
questions.explanation: Far more genes are carried by the X chromosome than the Y chromosome.

questions.scenario: Haemophilia is carried on the X chromosome.
questions.stem: Haemophilia is therefore a:
questions.explanation: Haemophilia is an example of a sex-linked disorder, with alleles carried on the X chromosome.

questions.scenario: A family tree shows how a genetic disorder is passed through generations.
questions.stem: This diagram is a:
questions.explanation: Pedigree charts are used to deduce patterns of inheritance of genetic disorders through a family.

questions.scenario: A scientist deduces a general pattern of inheritance from parts of a pedigree.
questions.stem: This is an example of:
questions.explanation: Drawing a general conclusion from some observations (a pedigree) is inductive reasoning; deducing individual genotypes from it is deductive.

questions.scenario: Human skin colour shows a smooth range of shades.
questions.stem: This continuous variation is due to:
questions.explanation: Continuous variation, such as skin colour, arises from polygenic inheritance and/or environmental factors.

questions.stem: Which is a discrete (discontinuous) variable?
questions.explanation: ABO blood group is a discrete variable with distinct categories; height and skin colour are continuous variables.

questions.scenario: A student displays height data showing the median, quartiles and outliers.
questions.stem: Which type of plot is best?
questions.explanation: A box-and-whisker plot displays outliers, minimum, first quartile, median, third quartile and maximum for continuous data.

questions.scenario: An organism has two different alleles of a gene.
questions.stem: Its genotype is:
questions.explanation: Heterozygous means having two different alleles of a gene; homozygous means two identical alleles.

questions.stem: Which trait is influenced by both genotype and environment?

options.text: A scar from an injury (environment only)

questions.explanation: Height is influenced by both genotype and environment; blood group is genotype-only and a scar is environment-only.

questions.scenario: A person has blood type A.
questions.stem: Which genotype(s) could give this?
questions.explanation: Blood type A results from IAIA or IAi, since i is recessive to IA.

questions.stem: The complete set of alleles in a population is called the:
questions.explanation: The gene pool contains all the alleles present in a population; an individual inherits only two of each gene.

questions.scenario: In peas, both male and female gametes form on the same plant.
questions.stem: This allows:
questions.explanation: Plants such as peas produce both gametes on one plant, allowing self-pollination and self-fertilization in genetic crosses.

questions.scenario: Alleles carried on X chromosomes are written with superscript letters.
questions.stem: This notation shows the letter on an uppercase:
questions.explanation: For sex-linked traits like haemophilia, alleles on X chromosomes are shown as superscripts on an uppercase X.

questions.stem: Many societies prohibit marriage between close relatives partly because:

options.text: It increases the chance of recessive disorders in offspring

questions.explanation: Close relatives are more likely to share recessive alleles, so their offspring are more likely to inherit recessive disorders.

questions.scenario: A student calculates the most common value in a data set.
questions.stem: This measure of central tendency is the:
questions.explanation: The mode is the most frequent value; mean, median and mode are measures of central tendency.

questions.scenario: A heterozygote (Tt) shows the same phenotype as a homozygous dominant (TT).
questions.stem: This is because the:

options.text: Dominant allele is expressed even when only one copy is present
Genotype has no effect on phenotype

questions.explanation: A single dominant allele is enough to produce the dominant phenotype, so TT and Tt look the same.

questions.scenario: Two genes on different chromosomes assort into gametes independently.
questions.stem: This independent assortment is a consequence of:

options.text: Random orientation of chromosomes in meiosis

questions.explanation: Independent assortment of unlinked genes results from the random orientation of chromosomes during meiosis.

questions.scenario: A dihybrid cross between two heterozygotes involves unlinked genes.
questions.stem: The expected phenotypic ratio is:
questions.explanation: A dihybrid cross between two heterozygotes for unlinked genes gives a 9:3:3:1 phenotypic ratio.

questions.scenario: A dihybrid test cross is carried out (heterozygote × homozygous recessive).
questions.stem: The expected phenotypic ratio for unlinked genes is:
questions.explanation: A dihybrid test cross of unlinked genes gives a 1:1:1:1 phenotypic ratio.

questions.scenario: The 9:3:3:1 ratio is based on Mendel's second law.
questions.stem: This law applies only if the genes are:

options.text: On different chromosomes or far apart on one
On the same chromosome close together

questions.explanation: Mendel's second law (independent assortment) applies only if genes are on different chromosomes or far enough apart for 50% recombination.

questions.scenario: A student explores gene loci in databases.
questions.stem: A locus is:

options.text: The position of a gene on a chromosome

questions.explanation: A locus is the position of a gene on a chromosome; students explore genes and their polypeptide products in databases.

questions.scenario: Two genes are located close together on the same chromosome.
questions.stem: These genes are said to be:
questions.explanation: Genes close together on the same chromosome are linked and tend not to assort independently.

questions.stem: Why can alleles of linked genes fail to assort independently?

options.text: They are on the same chromosome and tend to be inherited together
They are on the Y chromosome only

questions.explanation: Linked genes are on the same chromosome, so their alleles tend to be inherited together rather than assorting independently.

questions.scenario: In a cross, offspring show new combinations of parental traits.
questions.stem: These offspring are called:
questions.explanation: Recombinants have new combinations of alleles, arising from crossing over between linked genes or independent assortment of unlinked genes.

questions.scenario: Recombinants appear in the offspring of a cross involving linked genes.
questions.stem: Recombinants of linked genes arise from:
questions.explanation: For linked genes, recombinants arise when crossing over during meiosis separates the linked alleles.

questions.scenario: A chi-squared test is used on dihybrid cross data.
questions.stem: The chi-squared test compares:
questions.explanation: The chi-squared test compares observed and expected results to assess statistical significance.

questions.scenario: A chi-squared test uses a significance level.
questions.stem: The commonly used significance level is:
questions.explanation: The p = 0.05 level is commonly used as the threshold for statistical significance in the chi-squared test.

questions.stem: The null hypothesis in a genetic chi-squared test typically states that:

options.text: There is no significant difference between observed and expected results
There is always a significant difference
The sample is the whole population

questions.explanation: The null hypothesis states there is no significant difference between observed and expected results.

questions.stem: Independent assortment increases genetic variation by producing:

options.text: Many different combinations of alleles in gametes

questions.explanation: Independent assortment of unlinked genes produces many allele combinations in gametes, increasing variation.

questions.stem: The 9:3:3:1 ratio in a dihybrid cross reflects:

options.text: Independent assortment of two unlinked genes

questions.explanation: The 9:3:3:1 ratio arises from the independent assortment of two unlinked genes in a dihybrid cross of heterozygotes.

questions.scenario: In crosses involving linkage, alleles are shown alongside vertical lines representing:
questions.stem: The vertical lines represent:
questions.explanation: For linked genes, alleles are shown alongside vertical lines representing homologous chromosomes.

questions.scenario: In a chi-squared test on an F2 generation, the F2 is used as a sample.
questions.stem: This illustrates that statistical testing often:

options.text: Uses a sample to represent a population

questions.explanation: Statistical testing often uses a sample (here the F2 generation) to represent a population.
